questionText: Prove that for every integer $n$, the value $n^2$ leaves a remainder of $0$ or $1$ when divided by $4$.

steps.description: Choose an exhaustive split of the integers.
steps.workingLatex: n \text{ is even or } n \text{ is odd}
steps.explanation: Every integer is either even or odd, and nothing else. Splitting into these two cases is exhaustive, so proving the claim in each case proves it for all $n$.
An even integer is $2$ times some integer $k$; this lets us compute its square explicitly.
Squaring $2k$ gives $4k^2$, which is exactly $4$ times an integer.

steps.description: Read off the remainder for the even case.
steps.explanation: Because $4k^2$ is a whole number of fours, dividing by $4$ leaves nothing over: the remainder is $0$.
An odd integer is one more than an even integer, so it has the form $2k+1$.
Expanding $(2k+1)^2$ produces two terms with a factor of $4$ and a leftover $1$.
Factoring $4$ from the first two terms shows the square is a multiple of $4$ plus a remainder of exactly $1$.

steps.description: Read off the remainder for the odd case.
steps.explanation: Since $k^2+k$ is an integer, $4(k^2+k)$ divides exactly by $4$, leaving remainder $1$.
The even case gives remainder $0$ and the odd case gives remainder $1$; no other outcome is possible.
Because every integer is even or odd, these two cases account for all integers, so nothing has been missed.
For any integer $n$, its square leaves a remainder of $0$ or $1$ on division by $4$, as required.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: If $n=2k$ then $n^2=4k^2$ (remainder $0$); if $n=2k+1$ then $n^2=4(k^2+k)+1$ (remainder $1$). These cases exhaust all integers, so $n^2$ leaves remainder $0$ or $1$ modulo $4$. $\blacksquare$
